paper: VeriEvol: Scaling Multimodal Mathematical Reasoning via Verifiable Evol-Instruct

research_directions.title: Verifier-Aware Operator Composition Beyond Fixed Taxonomies
research_directions.seed_source: Section 3.2 limitation: 12-topic discretization reduced expressivity vs full cross product of (route × skill × answer-type × verifier-contract).
research_directions.gap: Current operators O_z are hand-assigned to fixed topic bins; this limits the diversity of evolved prompts and prevents the verifier's failure patterns from guiding operator selection.
research_directions.hypothesis: Learning to compose operators conditioned on both seed features and verifier failure history produces higher-quality training data than any fixed taxonomy.
research_directions.approach: Replace the 12-topic routing with a learned composition function f(seed_features, verifier_feedback) → operator_subset. Use verifier confusion matrices to identify which operator classes systematically fail on which seed types, and weight operators to compensate.

steps: 1. Run VeriEvol on 1K seeds with per-operator success tracking (what % pass φ_q × HTV-Agent)
2. Build a confusion matrix: operator class × seed feature (problem family, image complexity, answer type)
3. Replace 12-topic routing with a linear probe over seed features predicting per-operator success probability
4. Compare evolved data quality (SFT accuracy at 10K) against vanilla VeriEvol routing
minimal_viable_experiment.success_metric: +0.5-1.5 avg accuracy improvement at matched 10K SFT budget over vanilla routing
minimal_viable_experiment.risk: May overfit to the 1K seed distribution; validate on a held-out 200-seed pool

research_directions.title: Heterogeneous Verifier Ensembles Across Backbones
research_directions.seed_source: Limitation ii (Section 5): HTV-Agent accepts wrong answers when all channels share a blind spot; all use same backbone family.
research_directions.gap: Current HTV-Agent uses a single backbone family (Gemini variants); blind spots can be shared across channels because solver and verifier weights are correlated.
research_directions.hypothesis: Using different backbone families (e.g., Qwen + Llama + Gemini as solvers, Claude as decider) reduces shared blind spots more than adding channels within the same family.
research_directions.approach: Implement HTV-Agent with heterogeneous solvers (different architectures/training distributions) and cross-backbone decider. Measure shared blind-spot rate under homogeneous vs heterogeneous configurations.

steps: 1. Sample 200 seed problems and produce evolved prompts via VeriEvol operators
2. Run HTV-Agent in two configurations: (a) 3 × Gemini-3 solvers + Gemini decider, (b) 1 × Qwen2.5-VL, 1 × Llama-4-Vision, 1 × Gemini-3 solvers + Claude decider
3. Manual audit of 30 disagreements per configuration (180 samples total) — count shared blind spots
4. Measure acceptance gate rejection rate difference between configurations
5. Train VeriEvol-SFT-10K with both pools, compare accuracy
minimal_viable_experiment.success_metric: ≥30% reduction in shared blind-spot rate + at least statistically insignificant accuracy improvement
minimal_viable_experiment.risk: API costs × heterogeneous compute requirements; decoding stability varies across backbones

research_directions.title: Direct Matched-Budget RL Ablation of Verifier Contribution
research_directions.seed_source: Limitation ii.a (Section 5): Verifier on/off ablation not directly matched at RL budget — inference-time proxy on Gemini-3.5-Flash is indirect.
research_directions.gap: The +2.06 marginal verifier contribution is computed by comparing (RL-Evol + Verifier) against (RL-Evol) at different data volumes because matching the exact same RL data budget with/without verifier screening is expensive.
research_directions.hypothesis: When matched at the same RL data budget, the verifier's marginal contribution is ≥+1.5 (lower bound ≥ 75% of the reported +2.06).
research_directions.approach: Construct two 50K RL pools from the same 100K evolved-prompt candidates: (a) verifier-screened (accept ≈ 50K), (b) random subset without verifier filtering. Train GRPO on both with identical SFT init, measure accuracy gap. Repeat at 100K.

minimal_viable_experiment.compute: ~400 H100-hours (2 RL runs at 50K + 2 RL runs at 100K)
steps: 1. Generate 100K evolved prompts from seeds (using VeriEvol operators, no verification)
2. Run HTV-Agent on all 100K; collect accept/reject decisions
3. Construct pool (a): 50K accepted prompts. Pool (b): random 50K from the 100K (including rejected ones). Overlap controlled to at most 5K
4. Train GRPO on both pools from VeriEvol-SFT-Init. Repeat at 100K (all accepted vs all candidates)
5. Report accuracy on all 5 benchmarks
minimal_viable_experiment.success_metric: Verifier-screened pool ≥+1.5 avg accuracy over unscreened pool at both 50K and 100K
minimal_viable_experiment.risk: High compute cost; unscreened pool may have much higher ρ=1 failure rate, making the comparison noisy

research_directions.notes: This is the most important missing ablation. It's direct and expensive but definitive.

research_directions.title: Scaling to Larger Backbone Sizes (14B–72B)
research_directions.seed_source: Limitation v (Section 5): Single seed per RL run at 7B scale; results below ~1 point are suggestive at best. Backbone-capacity ceiling mentioned in error analysis.
research_directions.gap: The reported results are all at 7B. The backbone-capacity ceiling (~20% residual errors from multi-step symbolic chains) suggests VeriEvol's data quality gains may amplify at larger scales where the policy can actually exploit harder evolved prompts.
research_directions.hypothesis: VeriEvol's marginal gain over un-evolved RL increases with backbone capacity, because larger models can better exploit the hard image-grounded prompts that simpler models find intractable (ρ=0).
research_directions.approach: Repeat the main RL ablation (RL-Origin vs full VeriEvol) on Qwen2.5-VL-14B and 72B. Additionally, compute ρ(x) distributions for each model size to verify the rollout-curriculum intuition.

steps: 1. Run VeriEvol-SFT-Init on Qwen2.5-VL-14B (adapt 250K SFT recipe for larger batch size)
2. Train RL-Origin and RL-Evol+Verifier on 130K verified pool at 14B
3. Repeat at 72B (single evaluation run may be sufficient to establish trend)
4. Compute per-model-size ρ(x) distributions for the evolved pool
minimal_viable_experiment.success_metric: VeriEvol marginal gain increases from +3.88 (7B) to ≥+5.5 (14B) to ≥+8.0 (72B); ρ=0 fraction for hard prompts decreases with scale
minimal_viable_experiment.risk: Compute budget may be prohibitive; distributional shift in ρ may saturate by 14B

research_directions.title: Domain Transfer: Chart QA and Science Diagrams
research_directions.seed_source: Section 5 (Broader Impact): 'Composability principle suggests extensions to chart QA, diagram-based science, document understanding.'
research_directions.gap: VeriEvol is demonstrated only on mathematical reasoning benchmarks. The type-aware routing and verification framework is architecture-agnostic but not yet validated on other visual reasoning domains.
research_directions.hypothesis: The same two-axis framework (type-aware evolution + HTV-Agent verification) transfers to chart QA and science diagram domains with at least +2 avg gain over un-evolved baselines.
research_directions.approach: Apply VeriEvol pipeline to ChartQA and ScienceQA seeds. Adapt route-specific operators for chart types (bar, line, pie, scatter) and science diagrams (biological, physical, chemical). Measure transfer by training VeriEvol-SFT+RL and comparing against seed-only baselines on each domain's benchmarks.

steps: 1. Collect 5K seeds each from ChartQA and ScienceQA training sets
2. Define 3-5 route-specific operators per domain (chart-specific: 'extract value from axis grid', 'compare two series'; science-specific: 'count labeled structures', 'trace pathway')
3. Run VeriEvol pipeline: routing → evolution → HTV-Agent → D_SFT/D_RL
4. SFT on Qwen2.5-VL-7B at 10K/50K; compare against seed-only SFT
minimal_viable_experiment.success_metric: ≥+2 avg accuracy on each domain-specific benchmark over seed-only SFT baseline
minimal_viable_experiment.risk: HTV-Agent's visual-evidence module may need chart-specific adapters (legend parsing, axis alignment)

research_directions.title: Iterative VeriEvol: Closed-Loop Data Construction
research_directions.seed_source: Section 3.2 and Appendix F: Algorithm 1 operates statically on seed pool; no feedback from model's errors back into evolution.
research_directions.gap: VeriEvol constructs training data once, then trains. There's no loop where the model's errors (particularly VG failures that survive VeriEvol) become seeds for a second round of evolution.
research_directions.hypothesis: A closed-loop VeriEvol that feeds residual errors back as new seeds produces more efficient data (same accuracy with 30-40% less data) than a single-pass construction.
research_directions.approach: After training on VeriEvol data, collect the model's errors (especially VG type). Use these error-case images+questions as new seeds for a second pass through VeriEvol's operators (with adapted operator targeting). Compare accuracy vs same-budget single-pass baseline.

steps: 1. Train full VeriEvol (250K SFT + 130K RL) on Qwen2.5-VL-7B
2. Evaluate on MathVerse-VO; collect 200 error cases (any type), filter to VG+TS errors (~100 expected)
3. Run VeriEvol pipeline on these 100 error seeds → produce ~2K SFT + ~1K RL additional samples
4. Continue training from full VeriEvol checkpoint on the additional 30K-sample mixture
5. Compare accuracy against VeriEvol with same total budget but single-pass construction
minimal_viable_experiment.success_metric: Closed-loop VeriEvol matches or exceeds single-pass VeriEvol with 30-40% less total data (or same data but +0.8 higher avg)
minimal_viable_experiment.risk: Distribution shift from repeated VG error targeting may lead to overfitting on VG failure modes

research_directions.title: Cost-Efficiency Analysis: Optimal Verification Budget Allocation
research_directions.seed_source: Appendix B latency: HTV-Agent is 15-20× more expensive per sample than raw single call. D_SFT 250K + D_RL 130K implies substantial verification cost.
research_directions.gap: No cost-efficiency analysis of the verification budget. Does every sample need full HTV-Agent, or is a cheaper two-stage filter (cheap heuristic → expensive verifier only on borderline) more efficient?
research_directions.hypothesis: A cascade filter (cheap SC-only gate → full HTV-Agent only when SC confidence < threshold) produces 90%+ of VeriEvol's data quality improvement at ≤40% of the verification cost.
research_directions.approach: Implement a cascade verifier: (1) quick 2-solver consistency + rule-based check, (2) if confidence below threshold, escalate to full HTV-Agent. Sweep threshold at 10 cost-quality Pareto points. Compare against full HTV-Agent on all samples.

steps: 1. Run 3K prompts through full HTV-Agent; record all intermediate outputs (per-channel scores, decider confidence)
2. Simulate cascade by replaying: use subset of channels as cascade stages; sweep acceptance threshold
3. Identify optimal cost-quality Pareto frontier
4. Validate top-3 cascade designs by constructing 50K D_SFT and measuring RL accuracy
minimal_viable_experiment.success_metric: At least one cascade design achieves ≥95% of the full HTV-Agent accuracy gain at ≤50% verification cost
minimal_viable_experiment.risk: Phase transition: if the cheap stage is too aggressive, it may reject all hard-to-verify samples that actually benefit RL most

research_directions.title: Visual Grounding Necessity as a First-Class Optimization Target
research_directions.seed_source: Section 4.4: VG errors drop from 13→6 but remain the largest residual error category. Layer 2 analysis shows VeriEvol explicitly targets VG, but only through indirect scoring.
research_directions.gap: The current C_vis check (text-only probe) provides a binary signal (image is necessary or not). There's no gradient for how thoroughly the image is used, and no explicit optimization for image-grounded reasoning depth.
research_directions.hypothesis: Explicitly optimizing for visual grounding necessity (measured via a fine-grained metric like 'number of image regions referenced in trace') produces models with systematically fewer VG errors than indirectly targeting VG through prompt difficulty.
research_directions.approach: Augment the question gate φ_q with a fine-grained visual-groundedness score (using HTV-Agent's visual-evidence module to count distinct image regions referenced). Add a lightweight reward bonus during GRPO for traces that reference the image. Compare VG error rates under explicit vs implicit VG targeting.

steps: 1. Implement visual-groundedness scoring: for each evolved prompt, count distinct image regions referenced in the HTV-Agent trace (using OCR box counts + crop coordinates)
2. Augment φ_q with a threshold: reject prompts with VG score < 2 regions referenced
3. Construct 50K D_SFT (standard φ_q only) and 50K D_SFT (augmented φ_q)
4. Also try adding ρ_vg = VG_score as auxiliary reward bonus during GRPO (weight λ=0.1)
5. Compare VG error rates on MathVerse-VO and MathVision_MINI (60-sample audit)
minimal_viable_experiment.success_metric: VG error rate reduction: augmented VeriEvol reduces VG errors by ≥40% (relative) compared to standard VeriEvol
minimal_viable_experiment.risk: Optimizing for VG score directly may reduce prompt diversity; cross-validate that other error types don't increase
